content:
Moved from #general:
Daniel Geisler said:

Many topics are so far over my head it isn't funny. So can folks share how long have you worked with CT? Also a rough timeline of when you pick up different major concepts would be nice. Thanks, Daniel.

I discovered CT in Bert Mendelson's Introduction to Topology in my first year of undergrad. My mind was blown. I ran across the hall to try to articulate my excitement to my neighbour, who was also a mathematician (it was almost 10pm, and he was rather bemused at my excitement; he's in finance now, so I'm pretty sure I failed to capture his imagination). From then I read introductory textbooks in my spare time, and eventually did a research project on commuting limits and colimits between undergrad and Masters, in spite of the discouragement of certain professors regarding specialising in CT (including two algebraic topologists!). I'm learned what a topos was half way through my Masters, and I'm now in the second year of my PhD studying them.
Thanks!

I took a masters course in category theory (lectured by a physicist doing topos theory!) in 2010. Pretty soon after that I started using Haskell a lot so I was mostly playing with monads and related things. I started doing a PhD in proof theory and was super into categorical semantics of linear logic. Trying to understand the notes "categorical semantics of linear logic" by Melliès was maybe my first "serious" category theory (and I had a goal to understand some stuff by Hyland, but never got around to it). Around 2014 I gradually picked up string diagrams through a bunch of linguistics seminars and thought they were very cool but had too much hype. 2015 discovered open games and so started using monoidal categories and string diagrams full time. Started to actually use "serious" category thoery in my research in 2017, I rediscovered the category of lenses with another name (extracted from the foundations of game theory) and was studying it using fibred category theory. In 2018 I was involved in the first ACT school + conference, and I think it was at the conference that I decided that I ought to start thinking of myself as a category theorist now

I did my undergrad and master's in mathematics, and never heard the word category in 5 years from a mathematics professor. I first heard category theory mentioned in a Chomskian linguistics course I took for fun in my 3rd year (that'd be 2010). Then I did a bunch of master's courses on quantum information and computing with the only other people who used diagrams at the time besides Oxford, read about Bob Coecke & co in their bibliography. Google search and thought "cool, these people do quantum + linguistics + logic! Those are the things I like! Better learn some CT though if I want to do a phd there".
Downloaded and read Mac Lane and then Mac Lane-Moerdijk, really got into categorical logic (this would be 2012). Did a master's project on topos stuff, then went to Oxford in 2013, started on quantum stuff and then slowly became a higher category theory person.

So basically my timeline with CT is completely random! The people who should have naturally led me to categories didn't and the ones who shouldn't have did! I had to "leave mathematics" to become a mathematician.
Well, I seriously struggle with Mac Lane + Moerdijk even after 10 years of experience
Tbh I only read the “logic” part and hardly went into the “geometry” part.

Italian math exams often involve reading many chapters of a book in their entirety and understanding/learning every single proof, at the time I had a lot of stamina for that sort of thing. Now I find it very hard to be so systematic.
Like the bulk of the (oral) exams would literally be “state and prove the theorem about [whatever]”.
(Any result covered in the lectures could be asked.)

I did exactly the opposite of Amar: I got a PhD with Bob without knowing shit about category theory. That was 2015. I never even heard the word before. So my first PhD year was totally hellish, I had to pick up with a lot of stuff and it felt like everyone was speaking in an alien language. People recommended me a lot of useless books. I found them to be useless because they tried to aid understanding by being intuitive, but my intuition was set-theoretic at that point. So I'd read the first 200 hundred pages thinking I was understanding everything and then boom, like a puch in the face I'd realise I was back to square one. Finally I decided to give MacLane a shot (even if everyome was advising me not to) and that was by far the best category theory book I've ever read. Maybe much more difficult than others, but at least with it slow progress was real progress.
I basically got exposed to string diagrams first and to category theory later. My main research focus at the time was linguistics, which did not require a super heavy knowledge of cats. Then I started working also on quantum stuff and later on, when I joined Statebox, I switched to concurrency. Since then I learnt the theory behind petri nets and other concurrent structures, and then started diving into "real" category theory, mainly by reading MacLane-Moerdijk. That was illuminating since I've never been a geometry person (zero sense of spatial reasoning for instance), but that book gave me a lot of insight about "what geometry is about".
As a last thing: During these five years of CT I've developed quite a bad attitute towards reading books and papers. I find the language used there very difficult in aiding understanding. Usually a 15 minutes convo with the author of a paper gives you more insights and knowledge than trying to read the paper yourself for 2 weeks. So my policy in these last 5 years has mainly been going around bothering people and asking stuff like "hey, can you explain this to me?" I hope to not have annoyed to many people in this process. I still think this is the best way to learn.

I'm curious what's your opinion on the option of learning string diagrams before anything else? That's my preferred way to teach, but I didn't learn that way. To pure mathematically minded people I basically define a monoidal category as an algebra of the theory of string diagrams; and to everybody do exactly the same thing but without the fancy words, like Bob does
I wouldn't call that “the opposite”... it's quite similar except I caught up with some CT a few months before starting the phd :)
Also, like Jules, I started calling myself "applied category theorist" between 2017 and 2018, when I Started with Statebox. ACT2018 in Leiden only confirmed that was actually a thing. :D
I'm the opposite, I will make sure I look everywhere I can online and in the literature before I ask a question :weary:

To answer my own question, in the Nineties I read John Baez's This Week in Mathematical Physics and then Philippe Flajolet work with Sedgewick on Analytic Combinatorics. Before than I followed Stephen Wolfram's research and actually took over part of his agenda unifying maps and flows. About 2001 things clicked for me and I got the relevance of CT to my own research and the general idea behind umbral calculus. At the same time I put my math website up including a page on CT inspired combinatorics. See http://tetration.org/Combinatorics/index.html and http://tetration.org/Combinatorics/PartitionDiagrams.htm . Now my research is in general dynamical systems and I hope to connect that to CT.

It must have been around 2011~2012 after learning about group homomorphisms and continuous maps, I asked "what's going on with these functions between things that satisfy some condition? The response was something like "oh that's category theory, but I don't know much about it, so you'll have to ask someone else". This was at my community college, so there wasn't really anybody else to ask. Then when I got to UCR, I did an undergrad research project where my mentors were two of @John Baez's grad students, Brandon Coya and Jason Erbele. They taught me about categories, and I answered a question I had for myself for a few years about topology. Then when I started grad school, I attended a zillion seminars and classes with Julie Bergner, so I got a homotopical taste of category theory. Then I decided to work with John in like 2015~2016. So now I'm nearly done with my PhD in applied category theory.
gonna have to admit i got into category theory because i saw people talking about it in the haskell community :>
you hear people say "a monad is just a monoid in the category of endofunctors" as a joke enough times, u wanna know why that works :upside_down:

(Then, after you learn ""a monad is just a monoid in the category of endofunctors", you learn "a monoid in the category of endofunctors is just a monad in the 2-category Cat", and you are enlightened.)
then i went to a haskell hackathon and edward kmett drew me a commutative pentagon for the coherence of α and i stared at it really hard
that was probably the 2014 hac boston

Here's how that monad / monoid stuff works:

Before I had studied Zen for thirty years, I saw mountains as mountains, and waters as waters. When I arrived at a more intimate knowledge, I came to the point where I saw that mountains are not mountains, and waters are not waters. But now that I have got its very substance I am at rest. For it's just that I see mountains once again as mountains, and waters once again as waters.

(quote of Ch'ing-yüan Wei-hsin)
But it doesn't really take 30 years for monads!
:burrito:

I knew about categories since my first year of university I think, because I asked myself why so many things look repeated throughout algebra,and Wikipedia answered. I recall being so puzzled yet fascinated by limits and colimits. I didn't understand a thing though. Then as soon as I had a fairly free semester I decided to properly learn it, but that didn't happen until three years later, i.e. spring 2018. I struggled a lot to get myself in the right mindset, until one day everything clicked. I literally spent 10 hours in the library writing down everything I understood, it was wonderful. The rest is history.
when the category theory clicks :weary:
